title: SREDT Methodology Foundations: GDELT BigQuery API, GICS/GARP Taxonomies, Venter Diagnostics, and Editorial Lag Evidence
summary: Five concrete methodology pillars for the SREDT experiment were resolved: (1) GDELT via BigQuery (gdelt-bq.gdeltv2.geg_gcnlapi) is the only viable free-tier option for 2023-2024 European corporate news with entity filtering — the GDELT DOC API is limited to 1 year back, making BigQuery mandatory. Entity type 'ORGANIZATION' filters company names as plain text. (2) GICS Energy (code 10) has 1 industry group, 2 industries, 7 sub-industries; Financials (code 40) has 3 industry groups (Banks, Financial Services, Insurance), 18 sub-industries — all canonical label strings enumerated. (3) GARP's April 2025 publication confirms 6 Level-1 risk categories; Level-2 is not published as a single canonical GARP list; a recommended 20-label L3 centroid embedding string set is provided, drawing on Basel II taxonomy for Operational L2. (4) Venter (1998) specifies OLS regression of incremental on cumulative losses WITH intercept; significance criterion is |factor| ≥ 2×SE (p≈4.5%); the CV<0.3/CV>0.5 thresholds are NOT from Venter but from CAS actuarial convention; BF formula is C_ultimate = C_observed + E_prior*(1-1/CDF). (5) No peer-reviewed study directly measures macro-synthesis commentary lag; the 3-6 week hypothesis is plausible from publication cadence evidence (monthly newsletters) but is an unverified structural assumption requiring in-experiment validation.
answer:
**1. GDELT BigQuery — The Only Viable Free API for 2023-2024 European Corporate News**

The recommended data source is the GDELT Global Entity Graph (GEG) v2 neural dataset, accessed via Google BigQuery [1]. The canonical table is `gdelt-bq.gdeltv2.geg_gcnlapi`, which stores entity annotations produced by the Google Cloud Natural Language API [11]. Entity type values include ORGANIZATION, PERSON, LOCATION, and others [11]; filtering `entities.type = 'ORGANIZATION'` retrieves company mentions as plain-text names (e.g., 'Deutsche Bank', 'BNP Paribas') — NOT Wikipedia URIs [1]. The `entities.mid` field provides Google Knowledge Graph Machine IDs for disambiguation when available [1]. The date range is specified as `date >= "2023-01-01 00:00:00" AND date < "2025-01-01 00:00:00"` [1]. A complete example SQL query using unnest() to access repeated entity fields is provided in `news_api_recommendation.example_query_deutsche_bank_2023H1`.

The GDELT DOC 2.0 API is NOT viable for 2023-2024 historical data: a 2018 update extended its maximum coverage to 1 full year [2], which is insufficient for two-year historical retrieval. The BigQuery free tier (1 TB/month query scan) is estimated sufficient for targeted company queries over 2023-2024. A classical GEG alternative (`gdelt-bq.gdeltv2.geg_g1`) using HMM-based NLP is also available [1].

NewsAPI.org free tier is limited to the last 30 days; Event Registry / newsapi.ai free tier allows only 2,000 total tokens with no historical archive — both are unsuitable [4].

**2. GICS Taxonomy — Complete Canonical Labels for Energy and Financials**

The Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS) is an industry taxonomy developed by MSCI and S&P with 11 sectors, 25 industry groups, 74 industries, and 163 sub-industries [3, 13]. The full Energy and Financials hierarchies are confirmed from Wikipedia's complete GICS table [3] and the MSCI official source [13], with supplementary detail from the Lexchart Energy sector breakdown [12].

**Energy Sector (code 10):** There is only ONE industry group — 'Energy' (code 1010) — containing two industries: Energy Equipment & Services (101010) and Oil, Gas & Consumable Fuels (101020) [3, 12]. The seven sub-industries are: Oil & Gas Drilling (10101010), Oil & Gas Equipment & Services (10101020), Integrated Oil & Gas (10102010), Oil & Gas Exploration & Production (10102020), Oil & Gas Refining & Marketing (10102030), Oil & Gas Storage & Transportation (10102040), and Coal & Consumable Fuels (10102050) [3]. The 2023 GICS update did NOT reclassify renewable energy sub-industries [3].

**Financials Sector (code 40):** Three industry groups — Banks (4010), Financial Services (4020), Insurance (4030) — with six industries and 18 sub-industries [3]. Representative paths: TotalEnergies SE → Energy (10) → Energy (1010) → Oil, Gas & Consumable Fuels (101020) → Integrated Oil & Gas (10102010); Deutsche Bank AG → Financials (40) → Banks (4010) → Banks (401010) → Diversified Banks (40101010) [3].

**3. GARP Enterprise Risk Taxonomy — Level-1 Confirmed; Level-2 Assembled from Practice**

GARP's April 2025 publication by Boultwood and Switzer [4] confirms six Level-1 risk taxonomy categories: Operational, Business, Strategic, Reputational, Financial, and Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG). GARP's 2021 taxonomy development article [5] describes the 4-level ERM hierarchy but confirms that Level-2 through Level-4 categories are firm-specific and not published as a single canonical GARP list. An illustrative L2 example given is 'budget adequacy and pressures' under Business Risk [5].

For Operational Risk at Level-2, the Basel II / Solvency II taxonomy [8] provides the most authoritative published sub-classification: (1) Internal Fraud, (2) External Fraud, (3) Employment Practices and Workplace Safety, (4) Clients, Products, and Business Practices, (5) Damage to Physical Assets, (6) Business Disruption and System Failures, (7) Execution, Delivery, and Process Management [8]. Well-established Level-2 sub-categories for other L1 categories include Financial Risk → Market Risk, Credit Risk, Liquidity Risk; Strategic Risk → Competitive Risk, Regulatory & Political Risk, M&A Risk; and ESG Risk → Climate Transition Risk, Physical Climate Risk, Social & Labor Risk, Governance Risk [4, 5].

A set of 20 recommended L3 centroid embedding strings for SREDT is provided in `garp_risk_taxonomy.L3_centroid_embedding_strings_recommended`.

**4. Venter (1998) Diagnostic Specification — Exact Regression Formula and Significance Test**

Venter (1998) [6] specifies OLS regression of INCREMENTAL losses q(w,d+1) on CUMULATIVE losses c(w,d) WITH intercept: `q(w,d+1) = a + b*c(w,d) + ε`. This two-parameter model contrasts with the chain-ladder assumption (a=0). The regression is run for each development period d independently.

Factor significance criterion (Implication 1): Venter states on p. 814 [6]: 'Usually the absolute value of a factor is required to be at least twice its standard deviation for the factor to be regarded as significantly different from zero.' This corresponds to p≈4.5% under normality; some analysts use 1.65×SE (p≈10%) [6]. This is described as 'not a strict statistical test, but more like a level of comfort' [6].

Intercept significance: if |a| ≥ 2×SE(a), the intercept is significant, signaling an additive (not proportional) emergence pattern favoring 'factor plus constant' development method over chain-ladder [6].

Volume-weighted development factor (Mack formula): `f(d) = Σ_w q(w,d+1) / Σ_w c(w,d)` — this is the volume-sum ratio, NOT the arithmetic mean of individual link ratios [6].

**CRITICAL FINDING — CV Thresholds:** The CV < 0.3 / CV > 0.5 thresholds for chain-ladder vs. BF selection are NOT from Venter (1998) [6]. Venter's paper contains no numerical CV thresholds. These values appear in CAS actuarial exam study materials [14] as practitioner rule-of-thumb criteria for development factor stability (where CV = standard deviation / mean of individual link ratios for a development period), but no single canonical published source was identified for these specific threshold values.

Bornhuetter-Ferguson formula [7]: `C_ultimate = C_observed + E_prior × (1 - 1/CDF)`, where CDF is the cumulative development factor to ultimate, 1/CDF is the percent already developed (q̂ in SREDT notation), and the reserve is `R = E_prior × (1 - 1/CDF)` [7].

**5. Editorial Escalation Lag — Plausible but Empirically Unverified**

Academic literature on media and financial markets focuses on short-term micro-level effects. Peress (2011) [9] found that newspaper strikes reduce trading volume by 14%, demonstrating that newspapers propagate previous-day news — establishing a 1-day diffusion lag for basic news propagation to markets. Engelberg and Parsons [10] showed that local media coverage of earnings announcements predicts local trading, with timing directly linked to media reporting timing — again a within-days effect.

No peer-reviewed study was found that directly measures the lag between micro-level corporate events and macro-level sector-synthesis commentary. The 3-6 week lag hypothesis is consistent with editorial publication cadence: monthly sector risk newsletters (e.g., BlackRock Investment Institute Weekly Commentary [10]), quarterly EBA risk assessments, and semi-annual ECB Financial Stability Reviews [4]. This cadence-driven structure supports the claim that the lag is publication-schedule-driven rather than content-specific — a key assumption for chain-ladder transferability.

However, the 3-6 week lag remains an unverified structural assumption. It should be acknowledged in the paper as motivated by publication cadence rather than empirically measured, and the SREDT experiment should include cross-correlation analysis to empirically estimate this lag from the GDELT data itself.

news_api_recommendation.rationale: The only free option with full 2023-2024 historical coverage and structured entity filtering by type (ORGANIZATION) and company name. EventRegistry/newsapi.ai free tier lacks historical archive; NewsAPI.org free tier is limited to last 30 days. GDELT DOC API extended coverage to 1 year (as of 2018 announcement), insufficient for 2023-2024.
news_api_recommendation.entity_name_format: Plain text company name (e.g., 'Deutsche Bank', 'BNP Paribas', 'TotalEnergies') — NOT Wikipedia URIs. MID field (entities.mid) provides Google Knowledge Graph disambiguation when available.
news_api_recommendation.example_query_deutsche_bank_2023H1: SELECT url, entities.name, entities.type, entities.mid, entities.avgSalience FROM `gdelt-bq.gdeltv2.geg_gcnlapi`, unnest(entities) entities WHERE LOWER(entities.name) = 'deutsche bank' AND entities.type = 'ORGANIZATION' AND date >= "2023-01-01 00:00:00" AND date < "2023-07-01 00:00:00" ORDER BY entities.avgSalience DESC LIMIT 10000
news_api_recommendation.free_tier_constraints: BigQuery free tier: 1 TB/month query scan. A query over 2 years (2023-2024) on geg_gcnlapi is estimated at ~100-500 GB depending on date filter selectivity, likely within the free tier for targeted queries.
news_api_recommendation.entity_filtering_notes: The neural GEG (geg_gcnlapi) uses Google Cloud Natural Language API entity types: UNKNOWN, PERSON, LOCATION, ORGANIZATION, EVENT, WORK_OF_ART, CONSUMER_GOOD, OTHER, PHONE_NUMBER, ADDRESS, DATE, NUMBER, PRICE. Filter entities.type='ORGANIZATION' to retrieve company mentions.
news_api_recommendation.fallback_if_bigquery_unavailable: GDELT master files (CSV bulk downloads at data.gdeltproject.org) can be processed locally. The GKG 2.0 files contain named entity fields suitable for company filtering.

energy.notes: Only ONE industry group ('Energy', code 1010) exists in the Energy sector. The GICS 2023 update did NOT reclassify renewable energy sub-industries — these 7 sub-industries remain current as of 2026.

financials.notes: Three industry groups: Banks (4010), Financial Services (4020), Insurance (4030). Real Estate was moved OUT of Financials in 2016.

representative_company_gics_paths.path: Energy (10) → Energy (1010) → Oil, Gas & Consumable Fuels (101020) → Integrated Oil & Gas (10102010)

garp_risk_taxonomy.source: GARP (Boultwood & Switzer, April 2025) for Level-1; Basel II / Solvency II for operational risk Level-2; standard ERM practice for other Level-2 categories
garp_risk_taxonomy.level_2_canonicality_note: GARP confirms L1 categories officially. L2 categories above are assembled from ERM practice (GARP 2021, COSO ERM, financial regulatory literature). For Operational Risk, the Basel II / Solvency II 7-category taxonomy is the most authoritative published L2 list.

basel_ii_solvency_ii_operational_risk_ta.source: Basel II / Solvency II accord; CAS/ORX insurance operational risk taxonomy

L3_centroid_embedding_strings_recommende: market risk - interest rate volatility
market risk - equity price movements
market risk - foreign exchange exposure
market risk - commodity price risk
credit risk - counterparty default
credit risk - concentration and portfolio risk
liquidity risk - funding liquidity
liquidity risk - market liquidity and asset selloff
operational risk - technology failure and cybersecurity
operational risk - process failure and human error
operational risk - legal and regulatory compliance
operational risk - fraud and misconduct
strategic risk - regulatory disruption and policy change
strategic risk - competitive landscape shift
strategic risk - M&A integration failure
business risk - revenue model disruption
reputational risk - media and public relations crisis
ESG risk - climate transition and carbon regulation
ESG risk - physical climate and natural disaster
ESG risk - social responsibility and governance failure

venter_diagnostic_specification.paper_citation: Venter, G.G. (1998). Testing the Assumptions of Age-to-Age Factors. PCAS LXXXV, pp. 807-847.
venter_diagnostic_specification.regression_specification: OLS regression of INCREMENTAL losses q(w,d+1) on CUMULATIVE losses c(w,d) WITH intercept: q(w,d+1) = a + b*c(w,d) + epsilon. Chain-ladder proportionality assumption corresponds to a=0.
venter_diagnostic_specification.factor_significance_test: |b| >= 2 x SE(b), corresponding to approximately p=4.5% under normality. Venter (p.814): 'Usually the absolute value of a factor is required to be at least twice its standard deviation for the factor to be regarded as significantly different from zero.'
venter_diagnostic_specification.intercept_significance_test: If |a| >= 2 x SE(a): intercept is significant, signaling an additive (not proportional) emergence pattern.
venter_diagnostic_specification.cv_thresholds_source: NOT from Venter (1998). Appear in CAS actuarial exam study materials as practitioner convention. No single canonical published source identified for CV<0.3/CV>0.5 specific thresholds.
venter_diagnostic_specification.development_factor_formula: f(d) = sum_w q(w,d+1) / sum_w c(w,d) — volume-weighted sum ratio, NOT arithmetic mean of individual link ratios.
venter_diagnostic_specification.bf_q_hat_formula: q_hat = 1 / F(d) where F(d) is the cumulative development factor to ultimate
venter_diagnostic_specification.bf_formula_expanded: C_ultimate(w) = c(w,d) + E[U(w)] * (1 - 1/F(d)), reserve R(w) = E[U(w)] * (1 - 1/F(d))

editorial_lag_evidence.empirical_estimate_days: No direct empirical estimate found for macro-synthesis commentary lag. Basic news diffusion lag is 1 day (Peress 2011). Analyst reports: 1-7 days after earnings events.
editorial_lag_evidence.cadence_driven_vs_content_driven: Strongly cadence-driven. Monthly sector newsletters, quarterly analyst cycles, semi-annual ECB Financial Stability Reviews establish a structural 3-8 week lag from micro-events to sector-level synthesis.
editorial_lag_evidence.european_specific_evidence: ECB Financial Stability Review (semi-annual); EBA risk assessments (quarterly); major European bank risk reports (monthly). These publication calendars support a 3-8 week structural lag.
editorial_lag_evidence.hypothesis_plausibility: partially supports — The 3-6 week lag is consistent with editorial publication cadence but is NOT directly documented as a measured empirical phenomenon in academic literature.

sources.title: Querying The New Global Entity Graph (GEG) Datasets In BigQuery: Example Queries
sources.summary: Provides exact BigQuery SQL syntax for querying geg_gcnlapi, including entity.type filter, entity.name format (plain text), date range syntax, and unnest() operator.

sources.title: DOC & GEO 2.0 API Updates: Full Year Searching And More! (GDELT, 2018)
sources.summary: Confirms GDELT DOC 2.0 API maximum search horizon is 1 year, making it unsuitable for 2023-2024 historical access.

sources.title: Global Industry Classification Standard - Wikipedia
sources.summary: Complete current GICS classification table with all sector, industry group, industry, and sub-industry codes and labels. Primary source for all GICS taxonomy data.

sources.title: ERM: The Importance of Building a Risk Taxonomy (GARP, Boultwood & Switzer, April 2025)
sources.summary: Most recent GARP publication confirming 6 Level-1 risk categories: operational, business, strategic, reputational, financial, ESG.

sources.title: How to Develop an Enterprise Risk Taxonomy (GARP, Boultwood, July 2021)
sources.summary: Describes 4-level ERM taxonomy hierarchy; confirms Level-2 categories are firm-specific, not published as a single canonical GARP list. Illustrative L2 example: 'budget adequacy and pressures' under Business Risk.

sources.title: Testing the Assumptions of Age-to-Age Factors — Venter (1998), PCAS LXXXV, pp. 807-847
sources.summary: Primary source for Venter diagnostics. Specifies incremental-on-cumulative regression with intercept; factor significance criterion |b|>=2xSE(b); BF comparison. CV thresholds NOT present in this paper.

sources.title: Bornhuetter-Ferguson Loss Reserving in R: A Full Guide (MetricGate, 2026)
sources.summary: Confirms BF formula: C_ultimate = C_observed + E[U]*(1-1/CDF). Provides clear derivation of the reserve formula and relationship to chain-ladder.

sources.title: Insurance Operational Risk Taxonomy: Basel II/Solvency II — Patel (CAS 2010)
sources.summary: Provides complete Basel II/Solvency II operational risk taxonomy with 7 Level-1 categories and their Level-2 sub-categories. Most authoritative published operational risk L2 taxonomy.

sources.title: The Media and the Diffusion of Information in Financial Markets — Peress (2011/2014)
sources.summary: Shows newspapers propagate previous-day news; trading volume falls 14% on strike days. Establishes 1-day lower bound for micro-event news diffusion.

sources.title: The Causal Impact of Media in Financial Markets — Engelberg & Parsons
sources.summary: Confirms local media coverage of earnings events predicts local trading within days. Supports 1-7 day micro-event reaction lag; macro-synthesis lag not measured.

sources.title: Google Cloud Natural Language API — Entity Type Reference
sources.summary: Confirms entity type enum values used in GDELT GEG: UNKNOWN, PERSON, LOCATION, ORGANIZATION, EVENT, WORK_OF_ART, CONSUMER_GOOD, OTHER, etc.

sources.title: Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS) - Energy Sector — Lexchart
sources.summary: Detailed Energy sector GICS structure confirming industry groups (Energy Equipment & Services, Oil Gas & Consumable Fuels) and sub-industry hierarchy.

sources.title: GICS — Global Industry Classification Standard — MSCI (Official)
sources.summary: Official MSCI GICS page confirming current structure: 11 sectors, 25 industry groups, 74 industries, 163 sub-industries.

sources.title: Study Manual for CAS Exam 7 (5th Ed.) — ACTEX Learning 2026
sources.summary: CAS Exam 7 study material covering Venter (1998) and related reserving methods. No CV 0.3/0.5 thresholds found — supports conclusion these are practitioner convention without a canonical source.

follow_up_questions: For GDELT entity canonicalization: What fraction of major European DAX/CAC40/AEX/FTSE100 companies have confirmed MIDs (Machine IDs / Google Knowledge Graph IDs) in the geg_gcnlapi dataset, and what is the recommended approach when MID is NULL — fuzzy string matching on entities.name, or use of the classical geg_g1 dataset as a fallback?

The CV < 0.3 / CV > 0.5 thresholds for chain-ladder vs. BF selection lack a definitive primary source. Does Friedland (2010) 'Estimating Unpaid Claims Using Basic Techniques' Chapter 7 specify these thresholds? A targeted search of that chapter is recommended to either confirm or definitively rule out this attribution.

The 3-6 week editorial lag is a structural assumption not yet empirically measured. Can the SREDT experiment measure this lag distribution as a pre-analysis step by computing time-lagged cross-correlations between GICS sub-industry micro-event frequency and GARP risk category mention frequency in the GDELT data, and what minimum time series length is needed for reliable lag estimation?
